observations.recommendation:
We reiterated that the Local Chief Executive instruct 

(a) the Municipal Health Officer to 

(i) observe the instructions of the Department of Health Order No. 163 s.2002 in requesting and procuring drugs and medicines and ensure that only those drugs fall within the context of Philippine National Drug Formulary (PNDF) shall be requested and procured; and 

(ii) use generic name in all transactions related to purchasing, prescribing, dispensing and administering of drugs and medicines; 

(b) Bids and Awards Committee to 

(i) submit justification on the use of alternative mode of procurement of medicines above cited; and 

(ii) observe the guidelines and procedures prescribed in Republic Act No. 9184 in all procurement of the Municipality; and 

(c) Municipal Accountant to 

(i) coordinate with the former agency officials to facilitate the immediate submission of the required documents to the Office of the Auditor for proper/further evaluation of the transactions; and 

(ii) record the purchase of drugs/medicines using the appropriate inventory account pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-009.
observations.managementAction: The Local Chief Executive instructed the concerned officials to comply with the audit recommendations.
observations.reason:
No justification submitted. 

Some transactions were not procured in accordance to Republic Act No. 9184.

Lacking documents were not submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the management agreed that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer (MSWDO) to establish specific guidelines on the grant of assistance, such as the qualification of beneficiaries, amount and frequency of assistance, documentary requirements needed, and the like, concurred by the Sangguniang Bayan, pursuant to Section 2 of Presidential Decree No. 1445 to provide proper control in the disbursement of public funds.
observations.managementAction: The Social Welfare and Development Officer (MSWDO) submitted the specific guidelines on the grant of assistance concurred by the Sangguniang Bayan.

observations.recommendation: We commended the Management for its continuous efforts in complying with the COVID – 19 vaccination program guidelines, a way of providing protection to their constituents from adverse effects of the virus. We also recommend that the Local Chief Executive to instruct the Municipal Health Office to continue spearheading the LGU’s vaccination activities until the desired local herd immunity is attained.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality is committed to continue its efforts to comply with the COVID-19 vaccination program guidelines

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Assessor ensure that the set-up of RPT and SET Receivables and their corresponding deferred income accounts is in compliance with Section 20, Chapter 3 Volume I of the MNGAS.

We also recommend that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all necessary entries are properly recorded in order to reflect the correct balances in the financial statements.

Lastly, we recommend that the Municipal Accountant trace the entries made affecting these accounts and reconcile the differences by making the necessary adjusting entries.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant send confirmation letters from the creditors to validate the existence of the payables.  If non-existence of payable is warranted, revert the payables which have remained outstanding for a period of two (2) years or more.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant make the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to reflect the correct balances of the Cash in Bank and other affected accounts by debiting the Cash in Bank account and crediting the related accounts.

We recommend that the management direct the Municipal Accountant to maintain special accounts in the General Fund for the 20% Development Fund. Separate financial statements such as Balance Sheet, Statement of Income and Expenses and Cash Flows be prepared and presented as part of the schedules of the General Fund financial statements.
We recommend that the municipality ensure that the utilization of the LDRRMF is in accordance with the approved LDRRMP which fall under the thematic areas of the LDRRM.

We recommend that management require the accountable officers concerned to immediately liquidate their cash advances pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002. Issue demand letter on unliquidated cash advances and remind grantees on their responsibilities in the liquidation of cash advances within the required period. 

Likewise, institute measures to enforce settlement of outstanding cash advances through deduction from the salary of the official or employee who fails to comply with the requirement on the liquidation of cash advances.

We recommend that management require the Property Officer to conduct inspections before and after the repair of properties, prepare the corresponding reports and support the payment with Pre and Post Repair Inspection Report.

Also, furnish copies of the reports to this Commission within five days from the date of evaluation/ inspection.

We recommend that the management require the Local Finance Committee and the  Municipal Budget Officer to prepare a budget based on a reasonable estimate of income and monitor and control that disbursements does not exceed the released allotment.

Likewise, minimize the expenses of the LGU to at least break even by limiting total disbursements to fifty percent (50%) of the uncollected estimated revenue accruing to the fund in addition to the actual collections, provided that no cash overdraft shall be incurred, pursuant to Section 158 of the Government Accounting and Auditing Manual (GAAM), Volume I, in order not to incur net loss of current income.

We recommend that management require that the Trip Tickets be serially numbered and completely filled out with necessary details for the purpose of monitoring and controlling the use of government vehicles and consumption of fuel. 

Moreover, require the Municipal Drivers to prepare the Monthly Report of Official Travels for each vehicle duly certified by the driver and approved by the Administrative Officer and submit it to the auditor for verification.

Further, the Agency personnel concerned have to prepare a Report of Fuel Consumption and submit it to the auditor on a monthly basis in compliance with COA Circular No. 77-61 to determine the reasonableness of the Fuel, Oil and Lubricants Expenses.

We further recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:
Update the Taxpayer’s Index Card and reconcile the same with the records of the Municipality Assessor;
Submit the Taxpayer’s Index Card to the Municipal Accountant at the start of each year as basis in the recording of RPT Receivables; and
Actively implement tax collection methods and strategies to increase RPT collections to prevent bloating the balances of uncollected RPT in the books of accounts of the LGU.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Prepare the necessary journal entry to adjust the Government Equity and Due to NGA accounts of the municipality for the fund transfer received amounting ₱12,198,998.08 that were not yet supported with Liquidation Reports or FURs; and
Henceforth, recognize income only upon the submission of liquidation reports or FURs to the Audit Team and Source Agency.
observations.managementAction: Fund utilization report was submitted on March 31, 2023.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant:
Prepare the monthly Schedule of Unreleased Checks. Draw the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to restore the unreleased checks based on the schedule prepared by the Municipal Treasurer at the end of every year to fairly present the Cash in Bank balance of the LGU in the Financial Statements, and to reverse the entries of the unidentified reconciling items in the Cash in Bank account totaling ₱2,411,438.75.
Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer identify the reconciling items reflected in the Bank Reconciliation Statements to afford accuracy and reliability of the reported balances of the Cash in Bank accounts of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer prepared and submitted bank reconciliation schedules. The Municipal Accountant indicated that JEVs to restore unreleased checks will be prepared.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Require all End-User Units to submit all documents for the procurement of goods and services charged from the current year appropriation, the obligations of the suppliers having been duly fulfilled, based on the contract, during the current year, to facilitate the recording of the expenses in the books of accounts of the municipality; and
Record only the accounts payable which were obligated and have been completed.
We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer forward all unpaid valid claims to the Municipal Accountant’s Office for proper recording of the payables and expenditures incurred during the year in the books of accounts of the LGU.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the lacking JEVs within 5 days from receipt of the memorandum, to confirm the validity of the recorded transactions.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant instruct the personnel in charge in the submission of the JEVs to cross check all approved JEVs based on the List of JEVs and ensure that all JEVs in the List are transmitted to the Audit Team monthly, so that all recorded transactions in the books of the municipality have sufficient basis.

We recommended that the Mayor, as Chairman of the Municipal Development Council, and the Municipal Planning and Development Officer:
Identify and include in the AIP priority projects which are ready for implementation in the current budget year to ensure optimal utilization of the 20% DF; and
Stop the inclusion of PPAs in generic terms in the AIP for all projects of the LGU to ensure full implementation thereof within the timetable indicated in the AIP.
We also reiterated our CY 2022 audit recommendations with modification that the Municipal Engineer in coordination with the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator:
Conduct a detailed engineering of all projects prior to its inclusion in the AIP which includes the preparation of the Program of Work with the Detailed Estimates and plans/specifications; Prepare the Program of Work with the Detailed Estimates and plans/specifications for the unexpended balance of the CY 2022 Construction of various barangay infrastructure project amounting to ₱256,000.00 to ensure its full implementation; and Submit to the Bids and Awards Committee the Program of Work with the Detailed Estimates, plans/specifications and Purchase Request within the timetable indicated in the AIP, taking into consideration the period within which the procurement process and project implementation will be completed.
We further recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee schedule the procurement of projects in accordance with the timeframe specified in the AIP to ensure completion of the projects before the end of the year.

We recommended that the BAC Chairman should submit the lacking documents (original or certified photocopy of the original, Mayor’s/Business Permit, and Income Tax Return) to the Audit Team within 3 days from receipt of this report, for further evaluation and without prejudice to the issuance of the Notice of Suspension/Disallowance, if warranted. We recommended further that henceforth, the Municipal Accountant should require the submission of all documents for emergency purchase, for attachment to the Disbursement Vouchers, prior to the approval of the disbursement as required under Section 4 (6) of P.D. 1445 and Appendix A of Annex H of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.
observations.managementAction: Some supporting documents were submitted; henceforth, the Municipal Accountant will require submission of all documents for emergency purchases.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant:
Prepare the revised LDRRMF Investment Plan the re-alignment of the current appropriation whenever re-alignments and/or augmentations of the current appropriation of the LDRRM Fund are made; Ensure that all realignments or augmentations of the LDRRMF be duly approved by the MDRRM Council through the issuance of a resolution; and Strictly monitor and evaluate the use and disbursement of the LDRRMF based on the LDRRMFIP to avoid suspensions/disallowance in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Health Officer record all donations-in-kind received by the RHU in the Summary/List of Donations Received; assist the Supply Officer in the inspection and acceptance of all donations-in-kind, and in the conduct of inventory of the remaining undistributed items, if any; maintain copies of the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances and its supporting documents and prepare and maintain a Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM; and that the Municipal Accountant, based on the Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM, make a disclosure in the Notes to FS as to the quantity received, issued and balance of all donation-in-kind received by the Municipality, with categorization as to food items, medicines and medical supplies, hygiene kits, and others.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Health Officer and Supply Officer prepared the Summary and inventory; assistance was provided by the Supply Officer in acceptance and inventory.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Health Officer submit the original copies of Attendance Sheets and/or submit explanation/justification why some of the Attendance Sheets were photocopies only; and why the actual quantity of procured meals did not correspond to the number of recipients (frontliners) shown in the Attendance Sheets. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant review disbursement vouchers as to the completeness and propriety of supporting documents before these are forwarded for approval and payment.
observations.managementAction: Municipal Health Officer submitted supporting documents and explanations and Municipal Accountant reviewed vouchers for completeness.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant require all End-User Units to submit all documents for the procurement of goods charged from the current year appropriation, the obligations of the suppliers having been duly fulfilled, based on the contract, during the current year, to facilitate the recording of the expenses in the books of accounts of the municipality; Record all valid claims chargeable against the appropriations in the year the expenses were incurred in the books of accounts of the Municipality and recognize payables for all unpaid expenditures as of yearend; and that the Municipal Budget Officer initiate the enactment of an Ordinance to make available the unexpended appropriation which were reverted to the surplus of the general fund should there still be payment of prior year’s unpaid obligations not recorded as payables, to avoid the erroneous charging of prior years’ expenditures to the current year’s appropriation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Treasurer:
Stop the practice of encashment of personal checks out of cash collections remitted by collecting officers and ensure adherence to laws and regulations regarding handling of cash among personnel of the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to avoid incurrence of cash shortages in the future; Require that all personnel in possession with funds and property owned by the Municipality should be a permanent employee and properly bonded in accordance with the provisions of the law, to protect the interest of the government in case of loss thru theft, robbery, or other fortuitous events.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer stop the encashment of personal checks out of cash collections remitted by collecting officers and ensure adherence to laws and regulations regarding handling of cash among personnel of the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to avoid incurrence of cash shortages in the future.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the:
MGSO to maintain WOABSPC and validate the correctness of the submitted report from the OMA; Municipal Accountant to record the changes in the carrying amount of breeding stocks, maintain WOABSLC and disclose in the Notes to Financial Statement the reconciliation of changes in the carrying amount of biological assets between the beginning and the end of the current period; and Strict compliance with the provisions of IPSAS 27 and MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant:
Prepare the revised LDRRMF Investment Plan re-alignment of the current appropriation whenever re-alignments and/or augmentations of the current appropriation of the LDRRM Fund are made; Ensure that all realignments or augmentations of the LDRRMF be duly approved by the MDRRM Council through the issuance of a resolution; and Strictly monitor and evaluate the use and disbursement of the LDRRMF based on the LDRRMFIP to avoid suspensions/disallowance in audit.

We recommended for the Municipal Accountant to submit the Bank Reconciliation Statements of the aforesaid 2 bank accounts and reconcile the BRS balance of the other 2 bank accounts with the book balances. 

We also recommended for the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to conduct immediate verification and reconciliation of records between the 2 bank accounts with unreconciled balances.

We recommended for the Municipal Accountant to make the following adjusting entries:

Drugs and Medicines Inventory	 813,413.43
    Government Equity	                     813,413.43

We also recommended for the MHO and other department heads concerned to furnish a document relative to any receipt of goods and equipment from other government or private offices, as well as its issuances to the Municipal Account for proper recording thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant coordinate with the implementing offices concerned in the verification of the balances of the subject inventory accounts as of December 31, 2022, and make the necessary adjustments in the books supported with RSMI.

We recommended the following courses of action:

1.	The GSO-Designate should submit immediately the RCPPE as of December 31, 2022 and ensure the timely submission of this report in the succeeding year.

2.	Secure the approval of the Sangguniang Bayan through a resolution authorizing the Municipal Mayor to execute a Deed of Donation or Memorandum of Agreement to transfer PPEs processed and used by other agencies;

3.	The Municipal Accountant shall make the necessary adjusting entries to derecognize from the books of the agency the PPE items that will be transferred to other agencies and to adjust the erroneous entries made on PPE and depreciation accounts.

4.	Ensure the full implementation of the “One-time cleansing of PPE accounts” and undertake all procedures as provided for in COA Circular 2020-006 to come up with reconciled and correct balances of PPE accounts in the financial statements.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following courses of action:

1.	For the Management:

a.	To inform all offices to submit immediately to the accounting office all disbursement vouchers and supporting documents of recorded accounts payable that are still in their possession.

2.	For the Accounting Office 

a.	To verify the validity of the documents submitted by the offices and recommend immediate payment and/or settlement of the accounts, if warranted; 

b.	To facilitate the proper dropping of invalid and unidentified payables from the book of accounts; and

c.	To Conduct a regular review and monitoring of all booked obligations to ensure that only valid claims remain in the books.

We recommended the following to the Management

a. 	 Refrain from granting cash advances to accountable officers who have not settled their previous cash advances in compliance with Section 89 of P.D. No. 1445, and to those personnel not connected or employed in the LGU.

b.	Enforce appropriate action against accountable officers with long-overdue outstanding cash advances to immediately liquidate or settle their accounts. For cash advances that are identified and qualified to be written off, expedite the request supported with the required documents as provided under COA Circular No. 2016-005.

c.	Implement an efficient and effective control procedure in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances in conformity with the guidelines set in COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We recommended the following:

1.	For the Disbursing Officer to draw separate cash advance for each half-month period wherein the total net payment of each employee could be released to him/her after the end of the payroll period, and to liquidate timely all her cash advances.

2.	For the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all Disbursement Vouchers processed and signed are supported with complete documentation and properly recorded.  Also, make a timely demand to all DOs to liquidate their cash advances on time. 

3.	For the Municipal Treasurer to regularly monitor the accountability of the disbursing officer as well as the timely liquidation of her cash advances.

4.	For the agency officials concerned to review and assess properly the bond requirement of each accountable officer, and stop granting cash advance in excess of the maximum cash accountability allowed in the bond.

5.	For the management to regularly and strictly enforce the control procedures in the granting and liquidation of cash advances as provided in COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997, that would safeguard government funds against risks of loss and misuse.

We recommended that management: 

1.	Adequately plan and thoroughly study the feasibility of each project before proposing it to be included as one of the projects to be funded under the 20% LGDF; 

2.	Ensure immediate action and exert additional effort for the timely implementation and completion of the mentioned projects; and 

3.	Ensure the full utilization of the appropriated amounts for the benefit of the constituents from the PPAs to be implemented under 20% LGDF.

We recommended that Management:

•	Direct the MDRRMO to submit immediately the lacking distribution sheets or any form of acknowledgment evidencing that the assistance was duly received by the intended recipients; and

•	Ensure that subsequent transactions pertaining to the distribution of any form of assistance/support must be supported with complete documentation.

We recommended that the Management:

1.	Direct the Municipal Accountant and other officers concerned to declare the said unexpended balance as part of the unappropriated surplus and shall be made available for other social services after subsequent enactment by the Sanggunian Bayan. 

2.	Direct the MDRRMO and MBO to coordinate with the MACCO on the implementation of projects and the utilization of the fund thereof for proper recording of accounts and transactions of the LGU.

We recommended for the Municipal Mayor to: 

1.	Direct the HRMO to create an employee manual that will define the job description, rights, and responsibilities, among others, of all the employees within the Municipality;

2.	Issue a uniform and clear-cut policy on the hiring of Job Order workers to strengthen internal controls pertaining thereto; and

3.	Evaluate and determine the appropriate personnel complement for each office.

We recommended Management to:

Enforce the immediate liquidation of the transferred funds to the different barangays by sending demand letters and requiring the barangays to submit a status report on the project implementation if the project is still not completed; and

We recommended Management to:

Require the immediate submission of documents from recipient barangays for the proposed projects to be implemented;

Ensure immediate action and exert additional effort for the timely implementation of the projects; and 

Reprogram idle funds from unimplemented projects to finance other priority projects.

We recommended Management to:

Reprogram the excess unused appropriation for other disaster preparedness and mitigation activities for inclusion in the revised Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) for CY 2022.

We recommended that Management submit the perfected contracts, Purchase Orders (POs), and supporting documents, including the Memorandum of Agreement (MOA), of projects, programs, and activities to be implemented by the LGU to the COA audit team in accordance with the deadline set forth under COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.

We recommended that management adequately plan and study the feasibility of each project before proposing it to be included as one of the projects to be funded by the 20% Development Fund, and to ensure proper coordination among concerned offices.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

Municipal Treasurer and other officials concerned to undertake effective, efficient, and aggressive tax collection strategies to improve collection. Prepare and serve NDs for all delinquent RPUs, prioritize those which are more than 10 years delinquent. If possible, coordinate with other offices that are regularly conducting field inspections, to help them in the distribution of NDs;

For the delinquent real property taxes with NDs already served, we recommend the management impose the legal remedies either by administrative auction thru levy on real property or judicial action, following the procedures authorized in the LGC, to enforce collection and to undertake the same expeditiously on a yearly basis to reduce amounts accumulated as receivables for delinquent taxes.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC Chairman, persons authorized to conduct Shopping/Small Value Procurement by the BAC and acceptance and inspection committee to undertake the following courses of actions in order to strengthen the internal control system of the LGU particularly on the preparation and execution of procurement documents: 


1. Ensure that the actual date of the acceptance and inspection of the items/goods procured as well as whether or not these are completely/partially delivered must be indicated. These data are required in order to have sufficient basis to demand from the supplier liquidated damages or penalty of  1/10 of 1% of the cost of undelivered items per day of delay, in case of the latter’s failure to meet the contract time/period; and   

2. Ensure that the RFQ is in prescribe the manner by which price quotations shall be submitted i.e., by sealed or open quotation, and the deadline for their submission. The ABC must be indicated and the price quoted by the supplier must be indicated as these data are required for examination, evaluation, and comparison and preparation of Abstract of Quotations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Management requires the Municipal Accountant to present fully all the required data in the face of the Statement of Cash Flows in accordance to the Standards and the Manual; and to reconcile the Net Cash Flows from Operating Activities to Surplus/Deficit and disclose the said reconciliation in the Notes to Financial Statements in compliance with Section 29 of the IPSAS No. 2.
We recommended that the Accounting Office shall use appropriate accounts for recording transactions to ensure the accuracy and reliability of reported balances in the Financial Statements.

We recommended the following: the Municipal Accountant and the GSO adheres strictly to the provisions on the proper recording and reporting of transactions which requires the LGU to book all regular purchases of supplies through the inventory account whether it is consumed or not within the accounting period, maintain the Inventory Records using the Perpetual Inventory Method and continually update them for every addition and subtraction from the inventory and ensure the proper maintenance of subsidiary ledger cards for control and monitoring purposes. Moreover, reviews and revitalizes the composition of the Inventory Committee and fuels them up to religiously conduct the required annual physical count of unused/undistributed supplies and materials.

We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer to (i) comply with the provisions of IPSA 24 on the preparation of a Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts; (ii) reconcile their records on allotments and obligations monthly so that any resulting discrepancies can be immediately verified and corrected; and the Municipal Accountant to provide reconciliation and disclosures on the face of the SCBAA and/or in the Notes to FS the (i) changes between the original and final budgets; (ii) material discrepancies between the budget and actual amounts; and (iii) reconciliation of actual amounts per SCBAA and per financial statements, to enhance the transparency of the Municipality’s financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant take up adjusting/correcting entries in the books on the advance payments of RPT and SET receivables collected in 2022. Likewise, follows the correct accounting entries regarding RPT transactions as instructed in the Manual.

We recommended that the Management stops the practice of charging the 20% development fund expenses which are not among the allowable expenditures as provided under Section 3.0 of DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 01-2020 dated November 4, 2020. The LGU officials should strictly adhere to the guidelines set under Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1-2020 of the DBM, DOF, and DILG in the appropriation and utilization of the 20% Development Fund to ensure its optimal utilization.

We recommended that the Management require the LDRRM Officer to formulate or propose programs that are achievable to ensure implementation within the year and ensure that the QRF is utilized for its intended purpose to maximize the use of the fund which is to respond and recover from the impact of disaster/catastrophe.

We recommended that the Management require the MDRRM Officer to immediately submit the Relief Distribution Sheets of all the related transactions above as proof that all available relief goods were actually and fully received by intended beneficiaries and strictly comply to attach all the supporting documents in the ensuing transactions.

We recommended that the Management ensures that the augmentation of savings is always supported by an appropriation ordinance and strictly adhere to Section 336 of RA 7160 to ensure the validity and legality of the newly programmed PPAs and their implementation.

We recommended the following:

Instructs the Municipal Treasurer to employ additional procedures that would fast track the preparation and approval of the disbursement vouchers so as not to cause delay; and

Observes the rules and regulations provided in the Local Government Code; and release all the shares to the barangay governments pursuant to the provisions of the LGC.

We recommended that the Management requires the concerned officials and employees to refund the excess amount of ₱176,040.00 and avoid granting traveling allowances more than what is allowed as stipulated in EO No. 77. Further, direct the Municipal Accountant to impose strict adherence to the pertinent provisions of E.O. 77 dated March 15, 2019.

We recommended that the Accountant should be complaint on the above provisions and initiate a coordinated effort with the Acting Treasurer in requiring them to set timeliness in adherence to the prescribed period of submission of reports to COA.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor directs the Department Heads concerned to immediately prepare a consolidated report on all management actions taken relative to the audit observations and recommendations included in the Annual Audit Report for CY 2021 using the prescribed AAPSI Form; and henceforth, strictly adheres to the prescribed period for the submission of the AAPSI as provided under the GAA.

We recommended that the Management requires the Accounting Office to submit all the missing supporting documents for review and reference in the audit and strictly comply with the provisions under Section 4 of PD 1445 and COA Circular 2012-01 dated June 4, 2012, by attaching a documentary checklist on the face of the DV and to ensure complete attachments before payment.

We recommended that the Management pays the unpaid cash gift and a year-end bonus of the four elected officials and two employees totaling ₱179,647.85, allots budget for the monetary benefits of the elected officials during their terms and strictly adheres to the provisions of DBM Budget Circular No. 2016-4 dated April 28, 2016, in granting the cash gift and year-end bonus.

We recommended that the Management direct the Accounting Office to strengthen the internal control system in the Municipal Accounting Office, particularly in the review of the computation of taxes from compensation, submit a report to BIR on the over-withheld taxes from compensation and request if a refund can still be made to the affected employees through a monthly deduction from the remittable amount of taxes per month; and collect from the concerned personnel the under-withheld taxes from compensation and immediately remit the same to the BIR to avoid penalties.

We recommended the following: a Disposal Committee should be created, pertinent documents should be prepared by the Disposal Committee to facilitate appraisal review of the unserviceable properties by the COA, including: a properly accomplished Inventory and Inspection Report for unserviceable properties, appraisal Report of unserviceable properties for disposal, and other related documents such as photographs of unserviceable properties for disposal. Moreover, file the application for disposal with the COA, pursuant to COA Circular 92-386.

We recommended that the Management immediately designates an employee who shall be a responsible person to facilitate the LTO renewal of registration and insurance of all motor vehicles owned by the Municipality. More so, apply and/or renew the registration of all serviceable motor vehicles of the municipality with LTO and contracts of insurance with the GSIS within the prescribed time to avoid additional expenses in terms of penalties and payment of damages to third-party liabilities in case of vehicular accidents.

We recommended that the Management requires the concerned officials to immediately submit the required Accomplishment Report, in adherence to the prescribed laws, rules, and regulations and to support the validity of the payment on CNA incentives. Likewise, directs the Accounting Office to check the completeness of the documentary requirements before processing such claims.

We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) for all the funds/bank accounts maintained by the Municipality with the authorized depository banks every month or upon the availability of the bank statements, obliges the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to conduct regular reconciliation of their records at least quarterly and adjust the verified reconciling items to avoid its accumulation in order to reflect correct and reliable account balances, and requires the Accounting and Treasury Offices to assess and review the nature of the uncashed checks whether these are stale or cancelled checks and prepare the necessary adjusting entries in the books of accounts.

We recommended that the Management directs the Municipal Accountant to submit all Bank Reconciliation Statements, duly reconciled with the cashbooks of the Treasury Office, together with the supporting documents within the prescribed period to effect the timely review and verification of the reported Cash in Bank balances.

We recommended the following that the Municipal Accountant and other concerned offices to coordinate and complete the inventory and accounting of the damaged PPEs due to the typhoon, the accountable officer of the damaged PPEs to submit Notice of Loss and apply for a relief from accountability to COA; and the Accounting Office to record the loss of properties in accordance with the GAM for LGUs.

We recommended that the Accounting Office properly and religiously monitor all the records of the LGU to ensure the accuracy and reliability of the reported balances in the Financial Statements and make any necessary adjusting journal entry to reflect the correct amount of the affected accounts.

For the current year’s erroneous entry, we suggest the correcting entry below:

Dr. Due to NGAs                 ₱ 10,510,669.00

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly applies the proper accounting principles in keeping and recording of accounts as provided in the Government Accounting Manual for LGUs and other regulations in relation to the Trust Fund transactions, in order to present fairly the correct balances in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Management ensure that augmentation of savings is always supported by an appropriation ordinance and strictly adhere to Section 336 of RA 7160 to ensure the validity and legality of the newly programmed PPAs and its implementation.

We recommended that the Management directs the responsible official/s of the Municipality to submit the Memorandum of Agreements (MOAs) of the above fund transfers. Henceforth, in the ensuing years that all MOAs covering fund transfers should be submitted to the Audit Team within the prescribed period for a timely and thorough audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and General Services Officer to: a) Investigate and reconcile immediately the discrepancies between the PPE balances per accounting records and RPCPPE of the Inventory Committee; and, b) Undertake collaborative procedure to ensure that all PPEs included in the RPCPPE are duly recorded in their respective records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a) Require the Inventory Committee to prepare a systematic and well-planned physical inventory taking, which could be done in a year-round basis, until all the Property, Plant and Equipment of the municipality have been accounted for and prepare the corresponding Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) and ensure that all the necessary data are provided thereon; b) The Appraisal and Disposal Committee to take immediate action to facilitate the appraisal and disposal of the unserviceable items in line with relevant provisions of PD 1445 and PPSAS 17; and comply with the preparation of IIRUP for the identified unserviceable items, thereafter, proceed to the disposal processes; and, c) Direct the General Service Officer/ Inventory Committee and Municipal Accountant to ensure that all PPEs owned by the municipality are duly recorded in their respective records and that the Property Cards (PCs) maintained by the Property Unit and PPELCs maintained by the Accounting Unit are reconciled.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer request authority from the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) to authorize the Local Chief Executive to transfer the completed projects of prior years to the respective beneficiaries and prepare the Invoice Receipts (IR) Gen. Form No. 30-A to support the Certificate of Turn-over and Acceptance of Completed Projects to be received by the recipient schools and barangays. 

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant draw the Journal Entry Voucher/s (JEV/s) for the dropping of the affected PPE accounts from the books with the following attachments: (1) Authority from SP; (2) JEV of the beneficiaries taking up the PPEs received by them; (3) Invoice Receipt (IR) and (4) Certificate of Turn-over and Acceptance of Completed Projects duly received by the recipients.

We recommended that the Heads of offices and employees in-charged in the distribution of food items and other supplies ensure that the distribution list is properly filled up and includes the following minimum data: the content and quantity of every item distributed, date of distribution, the printed name and signature of recipient, the related calamity and, the name of the barangay/purok official attesting to the distribution.

We also recommended that for the food items consumed by the isolation facility, where meals are prepared by the concerned LGU personnel, prepare and submit the relevant supporting documents as enumerated in this AOM to substantiate the actual consumption of such goods.
observations.reason: No reply received from concerned offices

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Development Council to review all the projects undertaken and to be undertaken, and come up with a concrete action to address the deficiencies noted. 

We also enjoined the offices concerned to address the root cause of the delay/non-implementation- the MEO if it pertains to the preparation of POW and other technical matters, to fast track its preparation, and the Bid and Awards Committee (BAC) if it pertains to procurement matter.

Likewise, we recommended that the MPDO closely monitor and evaluate the status of the implementation of projects.
observations.reason: Projects under continuing appropriations totaling P9.5M still not implemented.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the procuring officer record in the stock card the inventories received based on the Acceptance and Inspection Report (AIR) and submit to the Municipal Accountant (MACCO) copies of the AIR, Purchase Order (PO) and delivery receipts. Issue RIS upon issuance of inventories which shall be summarized monthly in the RSMI. The RSMI together with the supporting RIS shall be submitted to the local accountant within five days after the end of the month. 

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require periodic physical inventory of supplies and materials to be reported in the RPCI to avoid the occurrence of recording inventory when these were all consumed or used in the project or activity. 

We further recommended that the MACCO make the necessary adjusting entry/ies to correct the overstatement and henceforth ensure that inventory balances as of reporting date represents undistributed/unconsumed supplies/materials.
observations.reason: No RPCI submitted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC Chairman, persons authorized to conduct Shopping/Small Value Procurement by the BAC and acceptance and inspection committee to undertake the following courses of actions in order to strengthen the internal control system of the LGU particularly on the preparation and execution of procurement documents: 

Ensure that the actual date of the acceptance and inspection of the items/goods procured as well as whether or not these are completely/partially delivered must be indicated. These data are required in order to have sufficient basis to demand from the supplier liquidated damages or penalty of  1/10 of 1% of the cost of undelivered items per day of delay, in case of the latter’s failure to meet the contract time/period; and 

Ensure that the RFQ is in prescribe the manner by which price quotations shall be submitted i.e., by sealed or open quotation, and the deadline for their submission. The ABC must be indicated and the price quoted by the supplier must be indicated as these data are required for examination, evaluation, and comparison and preparation of Abstract of Quotations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instructs the MACCO to conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the receivables and send demand letters to concerned party/ies to enforce collection of dormant accounts.  Cause the application of appropriate sanctions against erring borrowers, if necessary.

We also recommended that the MACCO evaluates the status of the outstanding receivables and to request for write-off of accounts whose balances are inactive or non-moving for ten years or more and its settlement/collectability could no longer be ascertained in accordance with existing auditing rules and regulations.
observations.reason: Request for write-off returned due to incomplete documentation (PESO).

observations.recommendation: We recommended full compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances, otherwise, withholding of salaries of accountable officers and or officials/employees concerned who fail to effect prompt liquidation in accordance with existing rules and regulations shall be made.

observations.recommendation:
1.14 We reiterated our audit recommendations in CYs 2014, 2015, 2017, and 2021
with modification, that the Municipal Accountant:
1.14.1 maintain a complete and updated PPE and Real Property Ledger
Cards to ensure that all PPEs are properly accounted for and to easily
facilitate the reconciliation of the RPCPPE with accounting records;
1.14.2 Trace back the transactions in the PPE balance labelled as
unreconciled items from prior years to determine its nature and
prepare the appropriate adjustments in the books of the Municipality;
1.14.3 reclassify as inventories the items with acquisition costs that are below
the capitalization threshold of P15,000.00;
1.14.4 reclassify the incorrectly recorded PPEs to their proper account;
1.14.5 reconcile the PPELC with the RPCPPE wherein differences noted
should be verified and the corrections be made, where necessary; and
1.14.6 determine the cost of the various donated equipment and recognize
them as PPE in the books.

1.15 We also reiterated our previous recommendations, that the Municipal General
Services Officer:
1.15.1 Assist the Municipal Accountant in the determination of cost of the
donated equipment and submit their related documents upon receipt
to the Municipal Accountant for proper recording; and
1.15.2 ensure that Report of Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE) was prepared
and accomplished completely when the physical count is concluded.

1.16 The Municipal Accountant and Municipal General Services Officer were amenable
with the audit recommendations and assured strict compliance thereof.

3.14 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
3.14.1 Prepare the necessary journal entry to adjust the Government Equity
and Due to NGA accounts of the municipality for the fund transfer
received amounting ₱12,198,998.08 that were not yet supported with
Liquidation Reports or FURs; and
3.14.2 Henceforth, recognize income only upon the submission of liquidation
reports or FUR to the Audit Team and Source Agency.

3.15 During the exit conference, the Municipal Accountant agreed and assures strict
compliance with the audit recommendations.

4.8 We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepare and submit to the
Municipal Accountant a monthly Schedule of Unreleased Checks.

4.9 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
4.9.1 Prepare the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to restore the unreleased
checks based on the schedule prepared by the Municipal Treasurer at
the end of every year to fairly present the Cash in Bank balance of the
LGU in the Financial Statements, and to reverse the entries every
January of the ensuing year; and
4.9.2 Reverse the entries made to record the unidentified reconciling items
in the Cash in Bank account totaling ₱2,411,438.75.

4.10 Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal
Treasurer identify the reconciling items reflected in the Bank Reconciliation
Statements to afford accuracy and reliability of the reported balances of the
Cash in Bank accounts of the Municipality.

4.11 During the exit conference, the Municipal Treasurer assures the submission of the
monthly Schedule of Unreleased Checks. The Municipal Accountant was also
amenable to the audit recommendations and committed to identify the reconciling
items reflected in the Bank Reconciliation Statements.

5.11 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
5.11.1 Require all End-User Units to submit all documents for the
procurement of goods and services charged from the current year
appropriation, the obligations of the suppliers having been duly
fulfilled, based on the contract, during the current year, to facilitate the
recording of the expenses in the books of accounts of the
municipality; and
5.11.2 Record only the accounts payable which were obligated and have
complete supporting documents.

5.12 We also recommend that the Municipal Budget Officer forward all unpaid
valid claims to the Municipal Accountant’s Office for proper recording of the
payables and expenditures incurred during the year in the book of accounts of
the LGU.

5.13 In the exit conference, the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer
promised to conform with the audit recommendations.

6.9 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the lacking JEVs
within 5 days from receipt of the memorandum, to confirm the validity of the
recorded transactions.

6.10 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant instruct the personnel
in charge in the submission of the JEVs to cross check all approved JEVs based
on the List with the physical JEVs and ensure that all JEVs in the List are
transmitted to the Audit Team monthly, so that all recorded transactions in
the books of the municipality have sufficient basis.

6.11 The Municipal Accountant committed to submit the lacking JEVs and their
supporting documents and ensure that hereafter all JEVs and documents will be
duly submitted to the Audit Team.

7.12 We recommended that the Mayor, as Chairman of the Municipal
Development Council, and the Municipal Planning and Development Officer,
identify and include in the AIP priority projects which are ready for
implementation in the current budget year to ensure optimal utilization of the
20% DF.

7.13 We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer:
7.13.1 Conduct a detailed engineering of all projects prior to its inclusion in
the AIP which includes the preparation of the Program of Work with
the Detailed Estimates and plans/specifications;
7.13.2 Fast-track the implementation of priority projects identified for CY
2022 and in the subsequent years; and
7.13.3 Submit to the Bids and Awards Committee the Program of Work with
the Detailed Estimates, plans/specifications and Purchase Request
within the timetable indicated in the AIP, taking into consideration the
period within which the procurement process and project
implementation will be completed.

7.14 We further recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee schedule the
procurement of projects in accordance with the timeframe specified in the AIP
to ensure completion of the projects before the end of the year.

8.6 We recommended that the BAC Chairman should submit the lacking
supporting documents (original OSS and/or certified photocopy from the
original, Mayor’s/Business Permit, and Income Tax Return) to the Audit
Team within 3 days from receipt of this report, for further evaluation and
without prejudice to the issuance of the Notice of Suspension/Disallowance, if
warranted.

8.7 We recommended further that, henceforth, the Municipal Accountant should
require the submission of all documents for emergency purchase, for
attachment to the Disbursement Vouchers, prior to the approval of the
disbursement as required under Section 4 (6) of P.D. 1445 and Appendix A of
Annex H of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.

8.8 The BAC Chairman and Municipal Accountant promised to conform with the audit
recommendations and the above-mentioned rules and regulations on emergency
procurement.

9.12 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal DRRM
Officer:
9.12.1 prepare the revised LDRRMF Investment Plan whenever re-
alignments and/or augmentations of the current appropriation
LDRRM Fund and in the Special Trust Fund are made;
9.12.2 ensure that all re-alignments or augmentations of the LDRRMF be
duly approved by the MDRRM Council through the issuance of a
resolution; and
9.12.3 strictly monitor and evaluate the use and disbursement of the
LDRRMF based on the LDRRMFIP to avoid suspensions/
disallowance in audit.

9.13 The Municipal Mayor and the Municipal DRRM Officer were amenable to the audit
recommendations.

10.20 We recommended that the Municipal Health Officer record all donations-in-
kind received by the RHU in the Summary/List of Donations Received,
Distributed and Balances, duly supported with the (1) Acknowledgment
Receipts, (2) proof of receipt by and distribution to the beneficiaries not
necessarily in the required form, and (3) the inventory of remaining
undistributed items, if any; and submit a copy of the same to the Supply
Officer and the Municipal Accountant.

10.21 We also recommended that the Supply Officer:
10.21.1 assist the Municipal Health Officer in the inspection and acceptance
of all donations-in-kind, and in the conduct of inventory of the
remaining undistributed items, if any; and
10.21.2 maintain copies of the Summary/List of Donations Received,
Distributed and Balances and its supporting documents and prepare
and maintain a Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM.

10.22 Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant, based on the
Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM, make a disclosure in the Notes to
FS as to the quantity received, issued and balance of all donation-in-kind
received by the Municipality, with categorization as to food items, medicines
and medical supplies, hygiene kits, and others.

10.23 The Public Nurse, representing the Municipal Health Officer, was amenable to the
audit recommendations and assured compliance thereof.

10.24 The Municipal General Services Officer also commented that the report of donated
items were already prepared by their office and assured the Audit Team that the
issuance or distribution of all donated items like the COVID vaccines are closely
monitored and documented by their office. He also committed to maintain copies
of the summary/list of donations received, distributed, and balances and to prepare
the Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM.

10.25 The Municipal Accountant also commented that the disclosure in the Notes to FS
were not made due to non-submission of documents indicating that there are still
unadministered COVID 19 vaccines in custody of the RHU as of December 31,
2022. However, she assured that compliance of the recommendations will be made
and henceforth, disclosures will be duly made.

11.11 We recommended that the Municipal Health Officer submit the original copies
of Attendance Sheets and/or submit explanation/justification why some of the
submitted copies of Attendance Sheets were photocopies only; and why the
actual quantity of procured meals did not correspond to the number of
recipients (frontliners) shown in the Attendance Sheets; and

11.12 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant review disbursement
vouchers as to the completeness and propriety of supporting documents before
these are forwarded for approval and payment.

11.13 The audit recommendations were duly noted for compliance by the officials and
employees concerned.

12.13 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
12.13.1 Require all End-User Units to submit all documents for the
procurement of goods charged from the current year appropriation,
the obligations of the suppliers having been duly fulfilled, based on
the contract, during the current year, to facilitate the recording of the
expenses in the books of accounts of the Municipality; and
12.13.2 Record all valid claims chargeable against the appropriations in the
year the expenses were incurred in the books of accounts of the
Municipality and recognize payables for all unpaid expenditures as
of yearend.

12.14 We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer initiate the
enactment of an Ordinance to make available the unexpended appropriation
which were reverted to the surplus of the general fund should there still be
payment of prior year’s unpaid obligations not recorded as payables, to avoid
the erroneous charging of prior years’ expenditures to the current year’s
appropriation.

12.15 The Municipal Budget Officer commented during the exit conference that moving
forward the supplemental budgets will already be prepared before payment of
unrecorded payables or prior years’ obligations.

12.16 The Municipal Accountant also remarked that due to limited funds, the payment of
prior year’s obligations was allowed. However, she assured the Audit Team that
compliance of the audit recommendations will be duly made.

13.7 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and Head of the Human
Resources Management Office:
13.7.1 issue written guidelines on the standard procedures to be followed in
the hiring of JOs to ensure professional and impartial selection
process;
13.7.2 revise the format of JO contracts to indicate the specific tasks to be
performed by the JOs, the office of deployment, and other necessary
provisions to protect the interest of the Municipality and the person
being hired.
13.7.3 limit the hiring of JOs only to the most necessary and only to
complement the existing workforce of the LGU; and
13.7.4 ascertain that the rates indicated in the payroll is the same rate shown
in the contract.

13.8 During the exit conference, the Human Resource Officer informed the Audit Team
that the municipality is already in the process of drafting the written policy or
guidelines on the hiring of JOs. She also stated that data as to office assignment
and specific tasks expected to be performed by the JOs will already be stipulated in
the contract starting CY 2023. Likewise, the necessary personal records for
identification and qualifications purposes were already required for submission as
of date.

14.8 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor should discontinue the assigning
of Job Orders to perform the work of accountable officers particularly as
collectors and strictly adhere the provisions of Section 101 of PD 1445,
Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 and Section 3 of Civil Service Commission
Resolution No. 02-790 to avoid wastage and/or losses of government funds.

14.9 We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer shall see to it that all
personnel in possession with funds and property owned by the Municipality
should be a permanent employee and properly bonded in accordance with the
provisions of the law, to protect the interest of the government in case of loss
thru theft, robbery, or other fortuitous events.

14.10 The Municipal Treasurer commented during the exit conference that as of date the
JOs were no longer designated as collectors in compliance with the
recommendations made by the Audit Team.

15.7 We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer stop the practice of
encashment of agency checks out of cash collections remitted by collecting
officers and ensure adherence to laws and regulations regarding handling of
cash among personnel of the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to avoid incurrence
of cash shortages in the future.

15.8 The Municipal Treasurer promised to adhere with the audit recommendations.

1.8. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant and General Services Officer to:
a) Investigate and reconcile immediately the discrepancies between the PPE
balances per accounting records and RPCPPE of the Inventory Committee;
and,
b) Undertake collaborative procedure to ensure that all PPEs included in the
RPCPPE are duly recorded in their respective records.

2.16. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) Require the Inventory Committee to prepare a systematic and well-planned
physical inventory taking, which could be done in a year-round basis, until
all the Property, Plant and Equipment of the municipality have been
accounted for and prepare the corresponding Report on the Physical Count
of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) and ensure that all the
necessary data are provided thereon;
b) The Appraisal and Disposal Committee to take immediate action to
facilitate the appraisal and disposal of the unserviceable items in line
with relevant provisions of PD 1445 and PPSAS 17; and comply with the
preparation of IIRUP for the identified unserviceable items, thereafter,
proceed to the disposal processes; and,
c) Direct the General Service Officer/ Inventory Committee and Municipal
Accountant to ensure that all PPEs owned by the municipality are duly
recorded in their respective records and that the Property Cards (PCs)
maintained by the Property Unit and PPELCs maintained by the
Accounting Unit are reconciled.

3.9. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to:
a) Refrain from transferring the unexpended/unobligated balance of
DRRM-Capital Outlay from General Fund to the STF, and;
b) Ensure that unutilized amounts in the STF beyond five (5) years should
be reverted back to the General Fund pursuant to COA Circular No.
2012-002. As members of the Local Finance Committee, they should
refrain from certifying the funds as available for DRRM PPAs.

4.5. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to ensure that all cash advances, except for petty cash, should be
fully liquidated at the end of each year, and observe strict adherence to the
rules governing the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

5.8. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to ensure that disbursement transactions should be duly
supported with complete documentation, before its approval for payment.

6.7. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a) Municipal Accountant to impose collection of liquidated damages from
the suppliers who incurred delays in deliveries; and
b) BAC Chairman to ensure that the terms and conditions indicated in the
contracts are strictly complied with.

7.10. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive should ensure that
seminars and conventions attended is responsive to the organizational result
and development outcome, keeping in mind the prudent use of the scarce
financial resources of the LGU. Also, before approving the authority to attend
any seminar-workshop, the authority and competencies of the sponsoring
organization/agency should be accordingly verified.

8.7. We recommended for the Municipal Mayor the following courses of action:
a) Create an Oversight Committee to: (1) review the current fees and
charges imposed by the LGU; (2) compute the appropriation rates for
each services rendered by the LGU, and (3) submit to the Municipal
Mayor the proposed revisions of the rates and charges.
b) Require the appointed Chairperson of the Oversight Committee, to
recommend to the SB the immediate approval of the proposed revisions
of the LGU’s Municipal Revenue Code; and,
c) Require the Municipal Vice-mayor, as the Presiding Officer of the SB to
facilitate the immediate issuance of a local revenue ordinance for the
adoption of the revised rates on fees and charges of the LGU.

9.9. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer
to closely monitor the site preparation for the immediate implementation of
the Construction of Municipal Public Market. Henceforth, ensure that the
development projects to be funded out of the 20% Development Fund are
well-planned and procurement-and-implementation ready.

10.11. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and other members of the
GAD Focal Point System ensure the implementation of GAD-related PPAs
thru proper gender mainstreaming or attribution of GAD fund to the
programs, projects and activities of the LGU.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant together with the Municipal Engineer to establish the accurate carrying value of all infrastructure assets by reassessing its estimated useful life using the guidelines provided by the DPWH as reference. Thereafter, excluding the salvage value in the computation of depreciation, draw the necessary journal entries to reflect the corrections.

We recommended to the Local Chief Executive the following courses of action:
Require the Municipal Accountant to draw appropriate JEV entry to recognize the transferred assets, so that balance of the affected asset account is accurately presented in the financial statements as at December 31, 2022;
Instruct the Municipal Administrator to coordinate with all offices and conduct re-orientation on the proper procedure and action to be done upon receipt of any properties/inventories that were delivered directly to them; and
Communicate with PAGRO for further necessary actions in order to immediately settle the lacking documents in the recording of the transferred assets in the books of Municipal Government.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor enjoin the:
Municipal Accountant to exert effort to locate the supporting documents to prove the validity of the claims and to enforce payment thereof.  If found to be no longer payable or without valid claimants which are two-year-old or over and/or other undocumented accounts payable irrespective of age, it should be reverted to unappropriated surplus and funds can be used to finance various projects and programs; and
Retain copies of supporting documents for all accounts payable recorded.

We recommended that officials and employees approving the loan application sufficiently justify the incurrence of loan-related expenses, and for its action/ inaction that led to the loss of funds, otherwise the said use of government funds shall be disallowed in audit.
We recommended that the Municipal officials and concerned employees conduct appropriate planning, consultation with the local finance committee, and with the national government agencies with oversight functions in LGUs, before entering into loan contracts taking into consideration their net available funds and the benefits to be derived therefrom.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the above observations, we have recommended the following:

Create an inventory committee to conduct physical count of PPE. To ensure the success of the committee, all department heads are advised to instruct their members to cooperate in the conduct of physical count; and

The inventory committee shall prepare the Physical Inventory Reports to be submitted to the Property Officer for verification and reconciliation with PPE Ledger Cards and the Property Cards to be maintained by the Accounting and Property Offices, respectively.

The following provisions of Sec. 490 of Government Accounting and Auditing Manual shall also be emphasized:

“Inventories of supplies, materials and equipment. — Physical stock-taking is an indispensable procedure for checking the integrity of property custodianship. In all cases, the physical inventory-taking which is required semi-annually or annually should be regarded with importance.

For effective control of government property, the COA prescribes the following regulations:

Chiefs of agencies are required to take a physical inventory of all the equipment and supplies belonging to their respective offices at least once a year, unless otherwise determined by the COA Chairman in specific cases. Such inventory shall be made as of December 31 on General Form 41 (A) (Appendix 12) and submitted to the Auditor not later than January 31 of each year, unless extended by the Chairman, Commission on Audit, upon prior request of the chief of agency concerned. When the exigencies of the service permit, the taking of inventory should be in the charge of a committee of two or more employees designated by the chief of agency, including the property officer or custodian, depending on the extent of property accountability in a particular agency, to be witnessed by a representative of the Agency Auditor.

Xxx”

The required annual/semi-annual inventory shall be certified correct by the committee in charge thereof, attested by the representative of the auditor concerned and approved by the chief of agency in interest.”
observations.reason: The management was only able to conduct a partial physical count of PPE accounts. Reiterated as Finding No. 2 in this year’s Annual Audit Report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the procuring officer record in the stock card the inventories received based on the Acceptance and Inspection Report (AIR) and submit to the Municipal Accountant (MACCO) copies of the AIR, Purchase Order (PO) and delivery receipts. Issue RIS upon issuance of inventories which shall be summarized monthly in the RSMI. The RSMI together with the supporting RIS shall be submitted to the local accountant within five days after the end of the month. 

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require periodic physical inventory of supplies and materials to be reported in the RPCI to avoid the occurrence of recording inventory when these were all consumed or used in the project or activity. 

We further recommended that the MACCO make the necessary adjusting entry/ies to correct the overstatement and henceforth ensure that inventory balances as of reporting date represents undistributed/unconsumed supplies/materials.
We recommended that the MACCO conscientiously review the entries made by the accounting personnel so that proper accounts are used upon recording the liquidation of petty cash fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC to: 

1. Observe strictly the requirements of the procurement law on the use of the alternative mode of procurement following the procedures enumerated in the updated RIRR of RA 9184; 

2. Ensure that documents to be submitted by the selected suppliers are correct, complete and attached to the DVs for payment. 

We also recommended that the Municipal Accounting Office ensure that the DVs subject to its pre-audit is supported with complete and sufficient documentation, depending on the mode of procurement, as enumerated under the procurement law and COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that the BAC determine the appropriate mode of procurement for POL in their procurement plans based on the policies, conditions and procedural requirements of the implementing rules and regulations under the law.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor being the head of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council to be more prudent in the formulation of the LDRRMF-IP. Otherwise, similar transaction will be disallowed in audit.

observations.recommendation:

Cause the application of appropriate sanctions against erring borrowers, if necessary.

We also recommended that the MACCO evaluates the status of the outstanding receivables and to request for write-off of accounts whose balances are inactive or non-moving for ten years or more and its settlement/collectability could no longer be ascertained in accordance with existing auditing rules and regulations.

We recommended full compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances, otherwise, withholding of salaries of accountable officers and or officials/employees concerned who fail to effect prompt liquidation in accordance with existing rules and regulations shall be made.
We recommended that the officials and employees concerned adhere strictly with the provisions of DILG-DBM JMC No.2017-1 on appropriating and utilizing the 20% DF for development projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor being the head of the Solid Waste Management Board direct the concerned officials/employees and offices to prioritize the SWM-PPAs in line with its ten-year period plan through inclusion in the Annual Investment Plan and Annual Budget sufficient funds for its implementation.

We also recommended that the funded SWM-PPAs be immediately implemented.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor enjoin the MBO and the Municipal Engineer to fast track the implementation/completion of projects from prior and current years that have unobligated allotments.

Further, we recommended that the Local Development Council should optimize the utilization of the 20% DF through: (a) proper planning of project including timing of execution; (b) coordinated action from LGU officials for better result and (c) strict monitoring in project implementation to ensure accomplishment of the project.

We recommended that management intensify its efforts to have the Solid Waste Management Plan approved by the National Solid Waste Management Commission to achieve its vision of preserving our natural resources by proper disposal of wastes.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Local Chief Executive to 
(a) instruct the Inventory Committee to

(i) undertake complete physical inventory of all property, plant and equipment of the municipality; and

(ii) prepare the corresponding Report of the Physical Count of PPE for submission to the Office of the Auditor within the prescribed period;

(b) direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to 
(i) maintain updated PPE Ledger Cards;

(ii) allocate depreciation expense yearly to all Property, Plant and Equipment except Land, until fully depreciated; and

(iii) make necessary adjustment for over allocation of depreciation expense;

(c) oblige the ICO-Municipal Treasurer to 

(i) maintain property cards for PPE to account for the receipt and disposition of properties; and

(ii) issue Property Acknowledgment Receipt (PAR) to establish the accountability for properties; and

(d) require the ICO-Municipal Treasurer and OIC-Municipal Accountant to reconcile their property records after the conduct of physical count to establish correctness of balances of PPE accounts.
observations.managementAction: Recognition of yearly depreciation for PPEs and correcting entries for over allocation of depreciation expense were made during CY 2021.
observations.reason:
Physical inventory of all PPEs was not conducted.

No Report of the Physical Count of PPE was prepared and submitted to Office of the Auditor.

No PPE Ledger Cards were maintained.

Allocation of depreciation expense to all PPE was still not done.

The agency still not maintain property cards for PPE.

Issuance of PAR to establish accountability for the properties was still not being observed. 

No reconciliation of property records was conducted by the Treasury and Accounting Office.

Withholding of taxes from some payments of goods and services was still not made.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Bids and Awards Committee Chairman, Human Resources and Management Officer and General Services Officer/ICO-Municipal Treasurer to: 

(i) strictly comply with COA Circular Nos. 96-010 and 2009-001 requiring the submission of Purchase/Job Orders/Contracts and its supporting documents within five (5) days after perfection; and

(ii) furnish the Office of the Auditor copy of Notices of Deliveries within twenty four (24) hours from acceptance to ensure timely review/evaluation of the contracts and inspection of deliveries, respectively, on a sampling basis.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by the Management.
observations.reason:
The timely submission of copies of contracts and purchase/job orders was still not made.

The Management still failed to furnish the Audit Team of copy of Notices of Deliveries within twenty four (24) hours from acceptance.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Engineer to 

post signboards comprising relevant information of PPAs of the Municipality in either of the forms stated in Section 2.2.1 of COA Circular No. 2013-004 to notify the public of the project to promote transparency and accountability;

stop the practice of putting billboard/poster bearing the name and image of the local official beside the required signboard in complying with the provisions of Section 2.2.6 of the same COA Circular No. 2013-004.; and

(c) submit the required report such as the list of all on-going government projects/programs/activities (PPAs) and those that are to be implemented during the year and monthly monitoring report.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by management.
observations.reason:
Information relative to the PPAs of the government was not disseminated publicly through the required means/platform. 



Putting billboard/poster bearing the name and image of the local official beside the required signboard was still in the practice of the Municipality.

No list of all on-going government Projects/Programs/Activities was submitted to the Audit Team during CY 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the management require the immediate settlement of disallowances otherwise, cause the withholding of salaries of those employees determined to be liable and comply with the requirements stated in the Notice of Suspensions, to prevent maturing into disallowances.
observations.managementAction: The concerned officials partially refunded some of the disallowances through direct payment.
observations.reason: Payment was still made directly by the liable officials and no salary deductions for each liable individual were made for the identified disallowances.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendations:

We recommended that the Municipal Accounting Office continuously  coordinate
with its software provider to resolve the inconsistencies found  in  various
PPE accounts  and  ensure  that  the  amounts  presented  in  the  financial
statements accurately reflect the balances in its supporting schedules.

Recommendations:

We recommended the Management to:


     1. Direct the  GSO  and  the  MAO  must  establish  an  effective  and
        practical  system  that  would  ensure  the   swift   and   regular
        reconciliation of records of both offices about the  Municipality’s
        Property, Plant and Equipment;


     2. Conduct a complete physical count of all properties; and

     3. Direct the GSO to correctly provide all the necessary data required
        in the RPCPPE for proper identification of all PPE items.

Recommendations:

We recommended that Management submit immediately the lacking documents  for
us to make a decision in audit and strictly observe the  guidelines  on  the
grant of benefits, especially on the required documents to be complied  with
to preclude the issuance of audit suspension or disallowance.

Recommendations:

We recommended Management to:

     1. Require the recipient barangays to coordinate with  the  Accounting
        Office on the submission of the lacking documents;
     2. Enforce the immediate liquidation of the transferred funds  to  the
        different barangays by sending demand  letters  and  requiring  the
        barangays to submit a status report on the  project  implementation
        if the project is still not completed; and

     3.  Execute  a  Memorandum  of  Agreement  (MOA)  for  all  succeeding
        transactions involving fund transfers to ensure that  the  LGU  and
        the recipient agency are aware of their responsibilities.

Recommendations:
